sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Chief Complaint
Facial pain, characterized by stabbing and shooting sensations.
History of Present Illness
The patient reports experiencing stabbing and shooting facial pain for approximately two to three months, primarily in the right cheekbone area. The pain is often triggered by certain temperatures or cold exposure. Ibuprofen and tiger balm have not provided relief. The patient reports occasional headaches, but denies a history of migraines. He describes the pain as a severe ten on a scale, like being stabbed with an ice pick, although it often subsides within a few minutes to an hour. He notes that the pain is often episodic. He reports that his family doctor recommended a neurosurgical consultation.
Findings
Physical examination revealed normal facial symmetry and equal smile bilaterally. The patient reported numbness on the right side of his face, but not on the left. Needle testing revealed dullness on the left side and sharpness on the right side of the face. MRI revealed a small blood vessel abutting and potentially compressing the trigeminal nerve.
Assessment
The patient's symptoms and MRI findings are consistent with trigeminal neuralgia.
Plan
medical_concepts: numbness on right side of face

sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value: I will initiate treatment with tegretol, starting with a low dose, and monitor for potential side effects, including memory loss, tingling, and dermatologic reactions. I will also order genetic testing to assess the suitability of tegretol for the patient. Follow-up in one month to assess treatment response and adjust medication as needed. If pain persists after one month, we will discuss surgical options for nerve decompression.

medical_concepts: numbness on right side of face
